blogs.title: Collaborate in Real-Time: A Developer's Guide to CRDTs
blogs.description: Dive into Conflict-Free Replicated Data Types (CRDTs), the magic behind real-time collaborative apps like Google Docs, and learn how they enable seamless offline editing.
blogs.author: Sunil Khobragade
blogs.content:
The Challenge of Real-Time Collaboration

Building applications where multiple users can edit the same document simultaneously is incredibly complex. How do you merge changes made while users are offline or working concurrently? Conflict-Free Replicated Data Types (CRDTs) are one of the most robust approaches. They provide mathematically proven merge rules so that replicas converge to the same state without requiring locks or centralized coordination.

CRDTs come in state-based and operation-based flavors. For sequences (text), structures like RGA or LSEQ attach positional identifiers to characters so concurrent inserts can be ordered deterministically. Use battle-tested libraries (Automerge, Yjs) for production.

class GCounter {
  constructor(id, state = {}) { this.id = id; this.state = { ...state }; if (!(this.id in this.state)) this.state[this.id] = 0; }
  increment(n = 1) { this.state[this.id] = (this.state[this.id] || 0) + n; }
  value() { return Object.values(this.state).reduce((a,b)=>a+b,0); }
  merge(other) { for (const [k,v] of Object.entries(other.state)) { this.state[k] = Math.max(this.state[k] || 0, v); } }
}

blogs.title: Advanced Prompt Engineering for AI Agents
blogs.description: Go beyond simple prompts. Learn advanced techniques like Chain-of-Thought, ReAct, and tool definition to build more powerful and reliable AI agents.
blogs.author: Sunil Khobragade
blogs.content:
Unlocking the Power of LLMs

Prompt engineering guides LLMs to produce better outputs. Combine system instructions, examples, and constraints; when accuracy matters, pair LLM outputs with deterministic verification.

async function agent(prompt, tools) {
  if (prompt.includes('calc:')) {
    const expr = prompt.split('calc:')[1].trim();
    return tools.calc(expr);
  }
  return 'I need more info.';
}
const tools = { calc: (expr)=>{ try{ return eval(expr).toString(); }catch(e){return 'error';} } }

blogs.title: Securing Your Serverless Functions: A Guide
blogs.description: Serverless architectures introduce new security challenges. Learn how to protect your functions from common vulnerabilities like event injection, insecure dependencies, and excessive privileges.
blogs.author: Sunil Khobragade
blogs.content:
A New Security Paradigm

Serverless shifts the attack surface. Use least-privilege IAM roles, validate event inputs, avoid secrets in code, and scan dependencies. Monitor with structured logs and tracing.

exports.handler = async(event)=>{
  const body = JSON.parse(event.body || '{}');
  if (typeof body.username !== 'string' || body.username.length > 100) {
    return { statusCode: 400, body: 'invalid username' };
  }
  return { statusCode: 200, body: 'ok' };
};

blogs.title: The Edge is Calling: What Edge Computing Means for Web Developers
blogs.description: Move beyond the centralized cloud. Learn how edge computing runs your code closer to your users, delivering ultra-low latency and enabling a new class of high-performance applications.
blogs.author: Sunil Khobragade
blogs.content:
The Need for Speed

Edge runtimes reduce latency by running code near users. Optimize for startup time, avoid heavy native dependencies, and design data strategies for eventual consistency.

addEventListener('fetch', event => {
  event.respondWith(new Response('Hello from the edge', { headers: { 'content-type': 'text/plain' } }));
});

blogs.title: Modern CSS is Here: A Look at Container Queries
blogs.description: Break free from viewport-based design. Learn how CSS Container Queries allow you to create truly modular components that respond to their own size, not the size of the page.
blogs.author: Sunil Khobragade
blogs.content:
Beyond Media Queries

Use @container rules to adapt components to container size. This enables modular UI components that behave correctly regardless of parent layout.

.card { container-type: inline-size; }
@container (min-width: 300px) { .card { grid-template-columns: 1fr 2fr; } }
